content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J S1n:e1 • Suilc 620 • Sacr.imcnto, CA 95814-2129 
Gregory A. Cook 
1127 l l 1h Street, Suite 700 
Sacramento, CA 95814 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
August 14, 2012 
Re: 
Your Request for Informal Assistance 
Our File No. 1-12-110 
Dear Mr. Cook: 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the 
Political Reform Act (the "Act'').1 This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political 
Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (111 re Oglesby 
(I 975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Because your inquiry is general in nature and could involve varying 
sets of facts (see Regulation 18329(c)(4}(F)), we provide you with informal assistance.2 
QUESTION 
Does your occupation as a state-registered lobbyist preclude you from fundraising for the 
Foundation for State Legislatures (Foundation), a foundation run by the National Conference of 
State Legislatures (NCSL), which is a nonprofit educational foundation? 
CONCLUSION 
You are permitted to fundraise for the Foundation so long as your activities do not 
constitute "arranging" a gift as provided in the Act and Commission regulations. 
1 The Polilical Reform Acl is con1ained in Governmenl Code Sections 8 I 000 lhrough 91014. All slalutory 
references are to the Government Code. unless 01herwise indica1ed. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Section!. 18110 1hrough 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulacions. All 
regulatory references are lo Tille 2. Division 6 of the California Code of Regulacions. unless 01herwise indicated. 
2 Informal assis1ance does not confer the immunity provided by a Commission opinion or formal written 
advice. (Regulalion I 8329(c){3).) 


FACTS 
File No. 1-12-110 
Page No. 2 
You are a registered lobbyist. You have a long-standing person_al association with the 
National Conference of State Legislatures. NCSL is an organization whose membership 
includes all 50 state legislatures. NCSL is not a lobbyist employer. NCSL sponsors and 
conducts beneficial training and professional development opportunities for legislative staff on 
various issue areas throughout the nation. 
NCSL has an educational non-profit foundation, called the Foundation for State 
Legislatures, which has 501(c)(3) tax status. You would like to encourage interested parties, 
individuals and corporations to contribute to the Foundation. Furthermore, you would like to 
encourage the Foundation to establish an account within the Foundation dedicated to the support 
of California's legislative staffs educational and professional development opportunities. 
You would have no role in the administration of or have any participation in the 
disbursement decisions of the Foundation. Your participation would be limited exclusively to 
encouraging contributions to the Foundation, and advocating for the Foundation to create a 
specific fund within the Foundation dedicated to supporting California legislative staff. The 
Foundation would maintain total control over decisions related to the disbursement of funds 
raised. 
ANALYSIS 
Making a Gift 
The term "gift" is defined in Government Code Section 82028(a) as: 
"Any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that 
consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a rebate or 
discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made in 
the regular course of business to members of the public without regard to official 
status. "3 
Lobbyist Gift Limitations 
The Act prohibits state-registered lobbyists and lobbying firms from making gifts to 
specified state officials, including legislators and specific legislative staff, aggregating more than 
$10 in a calendar month, or to act as an agent or intermediary in the making of any gift, or to 
arrange for the making of any gift by any other person. (Government Code Section 86203.) 
J Section 82044 defines payment. in part, as any "rendering of . . .  services or anything else of value, 
whether tangible or intangible. " 


Lobbyist Arranging Gifts 
File No. 1-12-110 
Page No. 3 
Regulation 18624 elaborates on the prohibition against lobbyists arranging gifts: 
0A lobbyist 'arranges for the making of a gift' within the meaning of Government Code 
Section 86203 if the lobbyist, either directly or through an agent, does any of the following: 
(a) Delivers a gift to the recipient. 
(b) Acts as the representative of the donor, if the donor is not present at the occasion of a 
gift. This does not include accompanying the recipient to an event where the donor 
will be present. 
(c) Invites or sends an invitation to an intended recipient regarding the occasion of a gift. 
(d) Solicits responses from an intended recipient concerning his or her attendance or 
nonattendance at the occasion of a gift. 
(e) Is designated as the representative of the donor to receive responses from an intended 
recipient concerning his or her attendance or nonattendance at the occasion of a gift. 
(f) Acts as an intermediary in connection with the reimbursement of a recipient's 
expenses." 
From the facts you have provided it is unclear what specifically you intend to do to 
fundraise for the Foundation. Therefore, we cannot provide a definitive answer to your question. 
However, if any of your actions fall within those described in Regulation 18624, then it would be 
considered arranging a gift and prohibited under the Act. If your actions are not prohibited by 
Regulation 18624 it appears that you are permitted to fundraise for the Foundation. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
EGB:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Zackery P. Morazzini 
General Counsel 
/µ 
By: 
Emiko Burchill 
Executive Fellow
